---
title: "Chapter 1 — Bear Cub (Cape Nome placer Au)"
subtitle: "OCR + 3-D modeling of 24 paper drill logs from a family heritage mining claim"
---

[← back to portfolio map](index.qmd)

## What this is

Bear Cub Mining Claim is a four-patent placer claim on the Nome
paleo-beach gold deposits in western Alaska, held by the family since
the early twentieth century. Its drilling history covers three
operators across four form-types between 1919 and 1955, producing
twenty-four paper drill logs that had never been transcribed. This
chapter turned those logs into a structured geospatial dataset with
quantified resource estimates, a three-dimensional model of the
bedrock surface and pay zone, and a defensible chain of title back to
the original 1908 federal mineral patent.

![Bear Cub MS 1178 on satellite imagery, drill holes colored by pay-zone average grade.](data/derived/bear_cub_resource/fig_aerial_pay_zone_grade.png)

## What got built

**OCR pipeline.** Multi-engine with arbitration: Tesseract for
printed form headers, Hugging Face TrOCR for handwritten depth and
lithology columns, Google Cloud Vision for the red-pencil supervisor
corrections layered on top of the original ink, and Claude Opus
4.5 with structured-output JSON as the final arbiter. The four
upstream engines each produce a candidate per cell; Claude selects
the most plausible value given the column's expected unit, and flags
cells where the engines disagree more than the column's measurement
uncertainty for manual review.

**Schema-aware ingestion.** The four operator-specific form
conventions disagree about what the same field on the same physical
form means. Convention C (1936 Hammon field log) records weights
only at the sample level on a back page; the front page has color
counts but no weights. Without recovering and distributing the
back-page sample totals across intervals via a color-weighted
proportional rule, every Convention C hole comes back at zero grade
and the property-total resource is halved. The pipeline implements
the recovery rule as a post-OCR step. Full breakdown of how
historical placer drill logs encode their data lives at
[`research/bear_cub_placer_drill_log_anatomy.md`](https://johnsondevco.com/plans/ai-minerals-internal/notebooks/bear_cub/main.html#archive-and-conventions).

**Resource estimation.** Voronoi polygon block model: tessellate the
property's four-corner quadrilateral into cells around drill-hole
positions, multiply cell area × pay-zone depth × hole grade, sum
over cells. Monte Carlo wrapper propagates three uncertainty sources
(gold fineness, OCR transcription noise, bit-area uncertainty) over
1,000 samples to produce a 90% credible interval. The published
1962 operator estimate (Tweet, Bear Cub Mining Company) puts the
property total at 10,056 oz; our pay-zone polygon-block credible
interval sits well below that, with the gap traced to a generous
pay-zone definition the operator used.

**3-D model.** PyVista mesh of the implied bedrock surface and
pay-zone horizon, plus a fence diagram in profile view. The dark
band immediately above the bedrock surface shows the third
beach-line geometry the historic operators were chasing — five
specific holes sit on that line and account for most of the
property's gold.

![Fence diagram, west-to-east. Dark cells above the brown bedrock line are bedrock-contact pay.](data/derived/bear_cub_resource/fig_grade_fence.png)

**Drill-site recommender.** Gaussian-process regression on the 24
holes' (x, y) → predicted pay-zone grade, with a per-location
uncertainty estimate from the GP posterior variance. Two acquisition
functions: max-variance (pure information gain) and expected
improvement (against a 0.05 oz/yd³ target). Each returns a
defensible next-hole pick. See [Chapter 5](drill_planning.qmd) for
the full drill-planning treatment.

## Proving title

The claim is BLM Mineral Survey 1178 in the Cape Nome mining
district. Title rests on three documents pinning the property to
specific WGS84 coordinates and a specific legal owner:

- 1908 federal mineral patent (General Mining Law of 1872) with the
  metes-and-bounds surveyor's course descriptions.
- 2014 BLM re-survey (Scott McClintock, RLS 8904-3) re-locating the
  four boundary monuments under modern geodesy.
- Alaska state recorder's chain-of-title from the original patentee
  through to the current family ownership.

The four-corner reconstruction walks the historical metes-and-bounds
language to within 0.3 m of the 2014 corner monuments — the
round-trip residual on the affine transform that takes the local-feet
survey grid to modern WGS84.

## Interesting findings

**The historical record is internally inconsistent in ways that
matter.** Each row of each log has up to three different numbers
describing the same depth interval's gold content: the field
panner's color count + rule-of-thumb weight estimate, the
supervisor's bench-scale weight after Wells/Janin displacement
correction, and a green-pencil reprice updating the historical
pennyweight value to a later gold price. The three numbers describe
different things and the discrepancies are not noise — they encode
real measurement uncertainty plus successive valuation layers. The
OCR pipeline preserves all three columns + a derived discrepancy
flag rather than collapsing them.

**The pipeline generalizes to the rest of the Nome paleo-beach
archive.** Three concrete spinoff projects: a surveyor's toolkit
(four-corner WGS84 anchoring from a few GPS readings + a patent
description), a drill-log CLI (folder of historical mining-record
scans → structured CSV with provenance + confidence flags per
cell), and a broader dark-data archive ingestion framework (court
filings, surveyors' notes, title histories, family correspondence,
all into one linked dataset).

## Deeper notebooks

- [Full Bear Cub project notebook](https://johnsondevco.com/plans/ai-minerals-internal/notebooks/bear_cub/main.html) — pipeline, modeling, resource analysis with code.
- [Mining-engineer re-analysis](https://johnsondevco.com/plans/ai-minerals-internal/notebooks/bear_cub/mining_review.html) — per-hole grades, plan view, resource estimate framed for a placer engineer.
- [Internal review notebook](https://johnsondevco.com/plans/ai-minerals-internal/notebooks/bear_cub/internal.html) — caveats, TODOs, methodology audit trail.
- [Placer drill-log anatomy](https://johnsondevco.com/plans/ai-minerals-internal/notebooks/bear_cub/main.html#archive-and-conventions) — how mid-twentieth-century churn-drill logs encode their data (Wolff + Wells references).

[← back to portfolio map](index.qmd)
